title: Carved pear honey stirrer
subtitle: Small green-pear stirrer for a honey jar
excerpt: A short carved stirrer for a honey jar in green pear wood. Handle and bulbous tip in one piece, finished in raw linseed. A small first project that uses the same axe-to-knife progression as a full eating spoon.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for the axe-to-knife progression on small treen. Beeton's Book of Household Management on traditional honey-jar tools.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven timber, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools before it dries and hardens.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the Swedish school-craft standard for whittling and spoon-carving.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, cutting off the end of the workpiece into open air.
A short controlled cut pivoted by the off-hand thumb pressing on the back of the blade. Used for fine shaping where blade-travel is small.

content.text: A honey stirrer is the smallest piece of treen worth making: a short handle with a rounded knob at the tip, used to lift honey from the jar without dripping. The shape is a fat teardrop in profile, carved from a single piece of
green wood

. Pear is the recommended species: tight grain, carves cleanly, dries food-safe pale, takes a linseed finish well. This is a half-evening project for a new carver, and a useful one to make first because the shape is forgiving. There is no bowl to hollow and no fine detail; the work is all push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts on the outside of the form.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one; the dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

A short pear billet: 14 cm long, 4 cm across the flat face, 3 cm deep. Pear is a fruitwood: dense, fine-grained, pale cream when freshly cut, ageing to a pinkish honey tone with light. Windfall pear is the usual source. Community orchards and the local tree-surgeon's offcut pile are reliable: pear is rarely sold as lumber at any size, so finding it is a windfall question.
Apple is the close alternative with the same working properties; cherry is a good substitute when pear or apple are unavailable, with a warmer pink tone.
Tools
A carving axe for the rough shaping, a
sloyd knife
for the body of the piece, a leather strop, and raw linseed oil. No hook knife is needed; there is nothing to hollow.
Workspace
A stable seat, the work held in the four-finger grip with the cut path travelling away from the body. A carving stool or a low workbench at hand height. Good light from over the off-side shoulder.
Method
Default to the
push cut
throughout. Strop every 15 minutes of work.

Mark the stirrer outline on the flat face of the billet. From one end: a rounded knob 3 cm across, then a smooth taper into a handle 10 cm long that narrows from 2 cm at the shoulder to 1.5 cm at the end. Mark it lightly in pencil on both flat faces.

Axe the handle waste off in three or four stop-and-rest passes, working from the shoulder down to the handle end. The knob waste is much smaller; remove most of it now too, leaving 5 mm proud of the pencil line on every face. The axe takes the bulk; the knife refines.
Move to the
blank

on the lap. Refine the handle to its finished oval cross-section with push cuts: thumb-push grip on the knife, blade angled along the handle, lifting thin curls of wood that drop away as you go. Aim for an oval 12 mm thick and 18 mm wide at the shoulder, narrowing to 10 by 14 mm at the end.
Round the knob with
thumb-pivot cuts

. The off-hand thumb sits on the spine of the knife, pressing forward in short controlled arcs. Work around the knob in eighths: cut a flat facet from each face, then rotate and repeat until the knob is even and round on every plane. Eight facets become a smooth curve once you soften the corners.

Refine the transition between handle and knob with thumb-pivot cuts. The shoulder should curve gently from handle to knob with no flat plateau; check by holding the stirrer in the hand and rolling your thumb across the shoulder. A flat spot will catch the thumb.
Check the cross-section is even by sighting along the length. Any asymmetry will become more visible as the wood dries. Correct now with light push cuts on the high side.
Leave the piece to dry slowly for 2 weeks on a shelf, out of direct sun and away from a radiator. Pear is dense and dries slowly without checking when wall thickness is even.
Finishing

Once dry, sand through 120 and 180 grit following the grain along the handle. Apply raw linseed oil liberally with a cloth, let it soak in for an hour, wipe off the surplus. The cure time before food contact is 2 weeks; the oil polymerises in the wood and is non-toxic once cured. Re-oil after every five or six washes.
Variations
Apple or cherry instead of pear: same method, same cure time. Apple is paler, cherry warmer in tone.
Longer handle for taller jars: extend the handle to 14 cm for a tall jar with a narrow neck. Keep the knob the same size.
Twin spiral handle: a more advanced finish where the handle is cut into a slow spiral with the sloyd knife. Adds an evening of work but turns a simple piece into a gift.
Care

Rinse the stirrer under warm running water after use; a small amount of washing-up liquid is fine. Towel-dry immediately. Never soak and never put through a dishwasher; the heat cycle pulls oil out of the wood and the soak warps the handle. Re-oil when the wood looks dull or feels rough.

items.cause: Cut depth uneven between facets during the round-over stage
items.fix: Mark the knob diameter with a pencil circle before facet-cutting. Take light thumb-pivot cuts and check by eye between each facet.

items.cause: Uneven wall thickness; one face thicker than the opposing face
items.fix: Sight along the length before drying. Take a light push cut from the high side to even the cross-section before the blank goes on the drying shelf.

items.cause: Cutting uphill on a slope of grain that runs out through the surface
items.fix: Read the grain before each cut. If a push cut tears, reverse direction and push toward the other end. Pear has a fine grain but still runs out at knots and at the shoulder.

items.symptom: Surface dulls after a few washes
items.cause: Linseed has worn off the active surface
items.fix: Re-oil with a small amount of raw linseed; the wood absorbs it in a few minutes. A fully cured stirrer drinks less oil than a fresh one.
